context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 8th April, 2024
Days Covered: 13 and 14 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Tree's Degrees [13 points]

Let T be a tree with n ≥ 2 vertices, of which k have degree at least 3.

(a) [8 pts] Prove by induction on n that T has at least k + 2 leaves.
    Hint: Pick a leaf.
(b) [5 pts] Prove the same result using the Handshake Lemma instead of induction.
    Hint: How many edges does a tree with n vertices have?


Problem 2. Froli-King [12 points]

(a) [6 pts] When moving on an 8×8 chessboard, a King can step to any of the ≤ 8 cells that are immediately adjacent to its current cell, either horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. It may be verified that there are 420 possible moves a King can make: 56 moves in each of the four compass directions (N, S, E, W), and 49 moves in each of the four diagonal directions (NE, NW, SE, SW).
Consider placing a King on an otherwise empty chessboard and performing a sequence of consecutive King moves. Say that this sequence is Frolicsome if it (a) starts and ends in the same cell, (b) never performs the same move twice, and (c) never performs a move that it has already done in the opposite direction. Conditions (b) and (c) can be said differently: if the king ever moves from cell c1 to cell c2, then he is not allowed to move from c1 to c2 OR from c2 to c1 for the rest of the walk.
Explain why there does not exist a Frolicsome sequence of King moves consisting of exactly 210 steps.
    Hint: Model this as a graph problem. What are the nodes and edges? Show that a 210-step Frolicsome sequence corresponds to an Euler tour of your graph.
(b) [6 pts] Show that there does exist a Frolicsome sequence of 196 King moves in this 8×8 board. (Show it exists, but don't actually construct it!)
    Hint: Find 14 edges to remove in order to make your graph Eulerian.


Problem 3. DAGs [12 points]

Answer the following questions about the dependency DAG shown in the diagram. Assume each node is a task that takes 1 second.

[Figure: a directed acyclic graph on nodes 1–12. Top row: 1 → 2 → 3, 4, 5, 6, with additional edges 2 → 6, 3 → 6, 5 → 6. Bottom row: 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, with edges 7 → 3, 8 → 3, 3 → 10, 9 → 10, 11 → 12, 10 → 12.]

(a) [2 pts] What is the largest chain in this DAG? If there is more than one, only give one.
(b) [2 pts] What is the largest antichain? (Again, give only one if there are more than one.) Prove there isn't a larger antichain.
(c) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks with a single processor?
(d) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks if there are unlimited processors available?
(e) [2 pts] What is the smallest number p of processors that would still allow completion of all the tasks in optimal time? Show a schedule for p processors that completes in optimal time, and prove that p − 1 or fewer processors must take longer.
(f) [2 pts] What is a topological ordering of these tasks? If there is more than one, only give one.


Problem 4. Pokémon Constraints [13 points]

Ash is selecting a team of Pokémon to use in an upcoming tournament. Each of his Pokémon has a number of types, shown below.

| Pokémon          | Types        |
|------------------|--------------|
| Altaria          | Dragon, Flying |
| Bidoof           | Normal       |
| Chikorita        | Grass        |
| Dratini          | Dragon       |
| Eevee            | Normal       |
| Floragato        | Grass        |
| Grovyle          | Grass        |

Ash has several constraints he must satisfy while selecting his team. We model this task as a graph problem. Let the propositions A, B, C, D, E, F, G be "(Altaria, Bidoof, Chikorita, Dratini, Eevee, Floragato, Grovyle, respectively) is on Ash's team". These propositions and their negations are called literals. Ash's first constraint is that his team must include at least one of Altaria and Eevee, expressed as the 2-clause (A ∨ E).

The two literals in a 2-clause needn't be distinct, and they can be negated. Ash's next two constraints are that his team must include Eevee, and that it cannot include Eevee without Dratini, expressed as the 2-clauses (E ∨ E) and (NOT E ∨ D). Ash's next constraint is that his team must include exactly one of Bidoof and Chikorita, expressed (along with the previous three constraints) as a formula in 2-conjunctive normal form (2-CNF):
    φ1 := (A ∨ E) ∧ (E ∨ E) ∧ (NOT E ∨ D) ∧ (B ∨ C) ∧ (NOT B ∨ NOT C)

(a) [2 pts] Fill in the blanks in the following 2-CNF formula with literals to express the constraint that no two Pokémon on Ash's team may share a type.
    φ2 := (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _)
[The five blanks correspond to the five type-sharing pairs: the Dragon pair, the Normal pair, and the three Grass pairs.]

(b) [3 pts] We can now convert the constraint formula φ := φ1 ∧ φ2 into an implication graph H = (V, I). V := {A, B, C, D, E, F, G, NOT A, NOT B, NOT C, NOT D, NOT E, NOT F, NOT G} is the set of literals, and I encodes the 2-clauses of φ as implications. A 2-clause (u ∨ v) can be written in the logically equivalent form (NOT u ⇒ v) ∧ (NOT v ⇒ u). For every 2-clause (u ∨ v) of φ, I contains the edges (NOT u, v) and (NOT v, u). For instance, I contains the two edges (NOT A, E) and (NOT E, A) to encode (A ∨ E). Draw H or list each vertex's out-neighbors explicitly.
    Hint: H is planar and has 19 edges.

(c) [3 pts] Let ψ be a 2-CNF formula, and let u, v be literals in ψ. Prove that if ψ is true and the implication graph of ψ contains a path π from u to v, then u ⇒ v.
    Hint: Induction on path length.

(d) [3 pts] Identify the SCCs of H (there should be 8). Draw the condensation graph of H, or list each SCC's out-neighbors explicitly.

(e) [2 pts] Find a topological order on the SCCs of H, and identify the set T of literals v for which [NOT v] precedes [v] in this order. Setting these literals to True gives a team that satisfies all of Ash's constraints! What is T, and what is Ash's team?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i'm stuck on pset 7 tbh—maybe Problem 1 first? i kinda get trees, but idk how the “pick a leaf” induction is supposed to work.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Before we dig into the 'pick a leaf' hint, let's make sure the foundation is solid. For an induction on n (number of vertices), what do you think the base case should be, and what does the tree look like in that case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting Problem 1(a) and wants help with the induction structure. I should use the Socratic method to guide them without giving away the approach. A good first step is to ask them what they already know about the base case and what 'picking a leaf' might do for the inductive step, rather than explaining it myself. I want to keep it bite-sized and see what they already understand.

exchanges.student: maybe n=2 since the problem says n≥2? then the tree is just 2 vertices with 1 edge, so both are leaves and k=0 i think.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So with k=0 leaves and 2 leaves in the tree, does the inequality 'at least k+2 leaves' hold in this base case? Once you confirm that, think about what the inductive step looks like: what does 'pick a leaf' let you do to the tree?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the base case n=2, recognized both vertices are leaves, and correctly determined k=0. I should affirm this and push them toward verifying the inequality holds in the base case, then move toward the inductive step.

exchanges.student: yeah, 2 ≥ 0+2, so base case works. for the step, i think i remove a leaf to get a smaller tree, but i'm not sure how k changes.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. When you remove a leaf, think about its neighbor — that neighbor's degree drops by 1. What are the possible degrees that neighbor could have had, and how does each case affect both the leaf count and k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right instinct about removing a leaf. The key insight they need to work through is how removing a leaf affects k (vertices of degree ≥ 3) and the leaf count. I should ask them to think carefully about what happens to the neighbor of the removed leaf, since that's where the interesting case split occurs.

exchanges.student: i think if the neighbor had degree 2, then it becomes degree 1 so it turns into a new leaf. if it had degree ≥4, then k stays same maybe? but if it had degree 3, then it drops out of the “degree at least 3” count, so k decreases by 1.
exchanges.tutor: Great, you've got the three cases right. Now apply the inductive hypothesis to the smaller tree in each case — remember, the IH says the smaller tree has at least k'+2 leaves, where k' is the new value of k. Does the inequality for the *original* tree hold in all three cases?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified all three cases. Now I need to push them to apply the inductive hypothesis to each case and check that the inequality holds in each. This is the core of the proof. I'll ask them to do that next.

exchanges.student: i'm a little lost on the counting tbh. like for degree 2 neighbor, the smaller tree has same k and at least k+2 leaves, and then adding back the removed leaf kinda keeps the number of leaves the same?
exchanges.tutor: Think carefully about what happens to the leaf count when you *remove* the original leaf in this case. You removed one leaf, but its neighbor *became* a leaf. So compared to the original tree, how many leaves does the smaller tree have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting confused about how the leaf count changes when adding back the removed leaf. I should ask them to think carefully: when we remove a leaf and its neighbor becomes a new leaf, how many leaves does the smaller tree have vs the original? This requires careful counting of what's gained and lost.

exchanges.student: ohh right, same number of leaves as the original. so if smaller tree has at least k+2 leaves, then original also has at least k+2.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly! That case is done. Can you now work through the other two cases (neighbor had degree 3, and neighbor had degree ≥ 4) the same way — tracking how many leaves the smaller tree has vs the original?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly worked out the degree-2 neighbor case. Now I should push them to work through the remaining two cases (degree ≥ 4 and degree 3) on their own, since they have the tools to do it.

exchanges.student: for deg 3, the smaller tree has k-1 high-degree vertices and i think one extra leaf compared to the original, so original leaves = smaller leaves - 1 ≥ (k-1)+2-1 = k. uh that seems wrong, can u help me spot it?
exchanges.tutor: Check your claim that the neighbor gains a new leaf. The neighbor's degree goes from 3 to 2 — is degree 2 enough to be a leaf? What does that mean for the smaller tree's leaf count compared to the original?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a sign error in their reasoning. They said 'original leaves = smaller leaves - 1' but the logic is backwards — the smaller tree has one MORE leaf than the original (the neighbor became a leaf after removal). So original leaves = smaller leaves - 1 + 1 = smaller leaves. Wait, let me think carefully: original tree has the removed leaf plus whatever leaves remain. When you remove the leaf, the neighbor (degree 3) becomes a new leaf. So smaller tree leaves = (original leaves - 1) + 1 = original leaves. So original leaves = smaller tree leaves ≥ (k-1)+2 = k+1 ≥ k+2? That's not right either. Let me re-examine. Original tree: some number L of leaves, k vertices of degree ≥3. Remove a leaf v. Its neighbor u had degree 3, now has degree 2, so u becomes a leaf in smaller tree. Smaller tree leaves = L - 1 (lost v) + 1 (gained u) = L. Smaller tree k' = k - 1. IH: L ≥ (k-1) + 2 = k+1. But we need L ≥ k+2. So this gives L ≥ k+1, not k+2. Hmm, that's actually a gap. Wait — but u was NOT a leaf in the original tree (it had degree 3), and it IS a leaf in the smaller tree. So smaller tree has L leaves (same as original). IH says smaller tree has ≥ k'+2 = (k-1)+2 = k+1 leaves. So L ≥ k+1, meaning original has ≥ k+1 leaves, not k+2. This seems like it only gives k+1. But wait — the removed vertex v was a leaf, so in the original tree we have L leaves. In the smaller tree we have L - 1 + 1 = L leaves. IH gives L ≥ k+1. So original has ≥ k+1 leaves, not k+2. This is a problem. Actually, I think the issue is that when the neighbor drops from degree 3 to degree 2, u becomes a leaf. So the smaller tree has the same number of leaves as the original (L). But IH only gives us L ≥ k+1, not k+2. Hmm. This suggests either the hint is to be more careful about what 'at least k+2' means, or I'm miscounting. Actually wait — maybe the insight is that u becoming a leaf in the smaller tree means the smaller tree has MORE leaves than a simple removal would suggest, so IH applied to smaller tree gives (k-1)+2 = k+1, but the original tree has the same number of leaves L = smaller tree leaves ≥ k+1. That gives L ≥ k+1, not k+2. This is indeed a gap in the bound for this case... unless we think about it differently. Actually, I think the resolution is that in this case, u was NOT a leaf in the original tree and IS in the smaller tree. So: smaller tree leaves = original leaves - 1 (removed leaf v) + 1 (u became leaf) = original leaves. And IH: smaller tree leaves ≥ (k-1)+2 = k+1. So original leaves ≥ k+1. But we need k+2. This seems like we're one short. I think the student needs to reconsider which leaf to remove, or whether this case is handled differently. Actually, looking at it more carefully: in a tree, if a vertex has degree exactly 3, removing one of its leaf-neighbors gives a tree where that vertex now has degree 2 (not a leaf). Wait, but the vertex u has degree 3 — it has 3 neighbors. One of them is the leaf v we're removing. So in the smaller tree, u has degree 2. That's not a leaf. So u does NOT become a leaf. I made an error earlier. Let me redo: smaller tree leaves = original leaves - 1 (v removed, it was a leaf). u goes from degree 3 to degree 2, so it does NOT become a leaf (degree 2 ≠ 1). So smaller tree leaves = L - 1. Smaller tree k' = k - 1 (u dropped out). IH: L - 1 ≥ (k-1) + 2 = k+1. So L ≥ k+2. That works! I was confusing degree 2 with leaf (degree 1) earlier. The student's confusion might be the same. So the student said 'one extra leaf compared to the original' which is wrong — the neighbor goes from degree 3 to degree 2, not to degree 1 (leaf). Only degree-2 → degree-1 creates a new leaf. So smaller tree has L-1 leaves (just lost v), k'=k-1. IH gives L-1 ≥ k+1, so L ≥ k+2. The student's error is thinking degree 3 → degree 2 creates a new leaf. I should ask them to reconsider what degree the neighbor has in the smaller tree.

exchanges.student: ah wait, no—degree 2 isn’t a leaf lol. so in the deg 3 case, the smaller tree has one fewer leaf than the original, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly! So now redo the inequality for this case: smaller tree has k'=k-1 and L-1 leaves, and IH says it has at least k'+2 leaves. What do you get for L?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that the smaller tree has one fewer leaf. Now I should push them to redo the inequality for this case using the IH, which they were trying to do before.

exchanges.student: ok, then \(L-1 \ge (k-1)+2 = k+1\), so \(L \ge k+2\). i think the deg ≥4 case should be similar with \(k'=k\) and \(L-1 \ge k+2\), so \(L \ge k+3\), which is even stronger?
exchanges.tutor: Perfect, all three cases work! You've got the base case and inductive step. Do you feel like you have all the pieces for a complete written proof now, or is anything still unclear before you write it up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked through all three cases of the inductive step. This is a good moment to consolidate and ask them to think about whether they have everything needed for a complete proof, before moving on.

exchanges.student: yeah i think i get the induction now. can i get a tiny hint for part (b) too—like how to set up the Handshake Lemma count?
exchanges.tutor: Sure. Two things to think about: how many edges does a tree with n vertices have, and what does the Handshake Lemma say about the sum of all degrees? Once you write those down, think about what you know about the degree of each type of vertex (leaves, 'normal' vertices, high-degree vertices).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is ready to move to part (b). The hint in the problem already points to the number of edges in a tree. I should ask the student to recall relevant facts about trees and the Handshake Lemma to set up the degree sum themselves, without giving away the approach.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic session on Problem 1(a). Every key reasoning step was elicited from the student rather than given directly: the base case, the three neighbor-degree cases, and the leaf-count arithmetic in each case. The tutor's hint for part (b) at the end was appropriately minimal, pointing to the two relevant tools without doing the algebra. No submission-ready content was produced, no off-topic drift occurred, responses were concise and well-targeted, and tone was consistently supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never stated conclusions before the student derived them; each turn posed a focused question that moved the student one step forward. The student did all key reasoning (base case verification, case analysis, inequality algebra). No near-submission-ready output was produced. 1.2: The tutor built directly on each correct student contribution (e.g., affirming the three cases in Turn 4, then immediately directing to the IH application) and diagnosed the specific confusion in Turn 7 (student's wrong claim about degree-2 being a leaf) with a targeted corrective question rather than a lecture. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback throughout, e.g., reminding the student to track leaf counts systematically across cases. 2.1/2.2: No repetition without progress; the conversation advanced steadily and stayed on-task. 3.1/3.2: Responses were short, interactive, and warm throughout.
